title: Slow Clients, Resource Limits, Backpressure, Admission, and Cleanup · Go Semantics Case: Admission Fairness Overload
summary: A server gives every connection a goroutine and unlimited buffer, refreshes deadlines on single-byte trickles, queues output without bounds, and retries accept failures until the process collapses. Planned artifact: Add and verify the admission fairness overload increment in connection resource budget and overload policy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Limit connections, active parsers, work, tenant share, and output while rejecting early and preserving fairness and health evidence.
Propagate slow peer writes through bounded queues, cancellation, deadlines, partial output evidence, and producer shutdown.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Admission Fairness Overload · Theory Admission Fairness Overload
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 3407 in a signed webhook receiver behind an HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 gateway. Build deterministic Go evidence to combine absolute phase time, memory, and output-queue budgets for slow-peer defense; preserve this operating constraint: one connection has exactly one read owner and one terminal close owner; then minimize a fuzz failure and add it to the deterministic regression corpus. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Admission Fairness Overload contract for the supplied data.
